content.text: TypedDict describes the shape of dictionaries with required and optional keys — ideal for chat messages, tool payloads, and API responses.

Optional and Union types model missing values and alternatives explicitly, eliminating a whole class of bugs when parsing model output, constructing function-call schemas, and threading state through multi-step agent loops.
) is a class-based syntax introduced in PEP 589 for declaring the expected keys and their value types in a plain Python dictionary.
At runtime a TypedDict subclass is just
— there is no wrapper object, no metaclass overhead, and no enforcement.

The value is purely in the annotation layer: mypy, pyright, and IDEs read the TypedDict definition and flag missing keys, extra keys, and wrong value types in code that constructs or reads those dicts.

union syntax is available directly. The two forms are equivalent:
in new code — it is more concise and readable without an import.

Together these tools form the foundation for precisely typing the JSON-like objects that flow through an agentic system: conversation messages, tool schemas, generation config dicts, and agent state snapshots.

LLM APIs communicate entirely through plain JSON objects with string keys. The OpenAI chat completion API, Anthropic's messages API, and every compatible provider use the same dict-of-strings shape for messages.
Without TypedDict, Python code that builds these dicts is opaque — a
signature documents nothing and catches nothing.
are clearly marked, and a typo like
triggers a mypy error before the code ever runs.

The Optional / Union dimension matters equally. Agent loops deal constantly with values that may or may not exist: a tool call may succeed or fail; a model may return text content or a tool call but not both; a config key may be overridden or absent.
forces every caller to acknowledge the None case. Static type checkers report an error if you try to use a
is required — exactly the narrowing discipline that prevents silent failures in production.
— TypedDict produces plain dicts, so they can be serialised with
and passed directly to any API client without conversion.
— editors that respect TypedDict annotations suggest valid keys when you write
, just like attribute access on a class.
— typos in keys, missing required fields, and wrong value types are caught at development time, not at 3 AM in production.
let you mark exactly which keys are optional rather than treating the whole dict as any-typed.
— TypedDict is purely a typing construct; it does not wrap the dict or add any runtime cost.
— TypedDict classes can inherit from each other, letting you layer on optional extension fields without repeating base definitions.
— because values are plain dicts, they are compatible with
, and any library that expects a mapping.
— TypedDict does not enforce keys or types at runtime.
passes silently; only mypy catches it. Use Pydantic when runtime guarantees are needed.
— mypy permits extra keys in TypedDict dicts by default (structural subtyping). In Python 3.13+ the
keyword seals a TypedDict; before that, extra keys are ignored silently.
makes every key optional. For mixed required/optional keys on Python < 3.11 you need the two-class inheritance trick.
Not a replacement for Pydantic at boundaries
— when receiving JSON from external sources (HTTP requests, LLM API responses, user input), Pydantic provides runtime coercion, validators, and clear error messages that TypedDict cannot.
values are not automatically usable as
guard or the checker reports an error.
Required and optional keys with total
By default all TypedDict fields are required (
To mix required and optional fields before Python 3.11, the conventional pattern is to inherit from a required base and an optional mixin:

from typing import TypedDict

class MessageRequired(TypedDict):
    role: str
    content: str

class Message(MessageRequired, total=False):
    name: str        # optional: display name for multi-agent scenarios
    tool_call_id: str  # optional: only present on tool result turns
to annotate individual keys independently of the class-level
This removes the need for the two-class inheritance pattern:

from typing import TypedDict, Required, NotRequired, Literal

class ToolCall(TypedDict):
    id: Required[str]
    type: Required[Literal["function"]]
    function: Required[dict]
    # These keys may be absent:
    index: NotRequired[int]   # streaming delta uses this
, the type checker treats it as the union of two types.
operation — inside the branch, the checker knows the concrete type.

content.text: — any dict that will be sent directly to an LLM API (
list) should be typed with TypedDict to catch missing or misspelled keys.
— the dict threaded through each step of an agent loop carries run metadata, message history, and optional fields like the active tool call. TypedDict makes the shape explicit.
) that are merged, overridden, and passed to APIs fit TypedDict naturally.
— any value that a lookup or API call might not return (tool result, error message, last completion text) should be typed
when you need instance methods, property access, JSON Schema generation, or runtime validation — TypedDict is not the right tool for those.
Upgrade to Pydantic at HTTP boundaries
— parse external JSON into Pydantic models at the edge; once validated and converted to internal TypedDicts, you can stay in plain-dict territory.
The recommended pattern is to declare all TypedDict shapes in a single
module that is imported throughout the project. This prevents circular imports and makes the schema visible in one place.

# types.py — shared TypedDict definitions for an agentic system
from __future__ import annotations
from typing import TypedDict, Required, NotRequired, Literal

# ── Basic message shape used by all LLM APIs ──────────────────────────────────
class Message(TypedDict):
    role: str        # "system" | "user" | "assistant" | "tool"
    content: str     # text payload

# ── Tool / function call embedded in an assistant turn ───────────────────────
class FunctionCall(TypedDict):
    name: str        # tool name, e.g. "search_web"
    arguments: str   # JSON-encoded argument dict (string, not dict!)

class ToolCall(TypedDict):
    id: str
    type: Literal["function"]
    function: FunctionCall

# ── Assistant turn that may or may not include tool calls ────────────────────
class AssistantMessage(TypedDict, total=False):
    role: str
    content: str | None      # None when tool_calls present
    tool_calls: list[ToolCall]

# ── Tool result turn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class ToolResultMessage(TypedDict):
    role: Literal["tool"]
    tool_call_id: str
    content: str

# ── Python 3.11+ fine-grained Required / NotRequired ─────────────────────────
class ToolCallPayload(TypedDict):
    name: Required[str]
    arguments: NotRequired[dict[str, object]]
    call_id: NotRequired[str]

# ── Top-level agent run configuration ────────────────────────────────────────
class GenerationConfig(TypedDict, total=False):
    model: str
    temperature: float
    max_tokens: int
    stream: bool
    stop_sequences: list[str]

Using total=False to mean "I'm not sure which keys exist"
is a deliberate design choice, not a workaround for uncertainty. Document the optional keys explicitly.

Using Optional[T] style when str | None is clearer

— mypy and pyright cannot introspect locally-defined TypedDicts as reliably as module-level ones. Define them at module scope.
Inheriting from TypedDict and a non-TypedDict base
— TypedDict classes can only inherit from other TypedDicts. Mixing with regular classes causes mypy errors.
Keep all TypedDict definitions in a dedicated
module and import from it. This creates a single source of truth for your schema.
(Python 3.11+) for mixed key optionality rather than the two-class inheritance pattern — it is clearer and less boilerplate.

Validate external JSON at the boundary using Pydantic, then convert to TypedDicts for internal use. This isolates the runtime cost to the edge.
in all new code — it is a Python 3.10+ union that requires no import.
TypedDict has zero performance overhead. At runtime a TypedDict subclass is exactly a
— no wrapper, no descriptor protocol, no metaclass machinery executing on access.
This makes TypedDict strictly superior to dataclasses or named tuples for hot paths that exchange large numbers of message dicts with LLM APIs.
Optional / Union types also carry no runtime cost. Python does not evaluate the annotation expression at access time — the union is only meaningful to static analysis tools.
is a single pointer comparison, the cheapest possible check in CPython.
— TypedDict dicts use the same memory layout as plain dicts. There is no per-instance overhead from schema storage.
works without any custom encoder because the value is a plain dict.
produce a shallow copy that is still a valid TypedDict value.
is the idiomatic O(n) merge and produces a plain dict; annotate the result back to the TypedDict type.

The table below compares TypedDict against common alternatives for structuring dictionary-like data in Python:
Typing optional fields explicitly improves reliability by forcing the code to handle all cases.
Consider a tool result field typed as
: if the tool call failed, the value is
, and any code that tries to process it as a
is caught by mypy before it reaches production.
Without the annotation, the same code would succeed during happy-path testing and silently fail at runtime when a tool errors.
TypedDict also reduces the risk of key-injection attacks that exploit poorly-typed dict merges.
When the merge helper is typed as
, the type checker flags any attempt to pass a dict with unexpected keys.
This is not a security boundary on its own, but it raises the difficulty of introducing unexpected fields through internal code paths.
are never placed into code execution paths without sanitisation; the type annotation alone cannot prevent misuse.
— never trust external JSON. Parse with Pydantic, then cast to internal TypedDicts. TypedDict annotations only matter after the external data has been validated.
— static checking eliminates a category of bugs (reading the wrong key) that would otherwise only surface in end-to-end tests or production.
for dictionaries — it declares which fields are required and which are optional, but does not enforce them at runtime.
— a signal that a lookup or step might not produce a result, and every caller must acknowledge that possibility explicitly.
of a dict (like the columns of a database table) and
The schema is checked by the compiler, not the database engine — fast at query time, verified at build time.
TypedDict annotates the shape of plain Python dicts without any runtime overhead.
Use TypedDict for LLM message lists, tool call payloads, agent state, and config dicts.
(Python 3.11+) or the two-class inheritance pattern on older versions.

Pair TypedDict (internal typed dicts) with Pydantic (external validation at API boundaries) rather than choosing one or the other.
